items.title:
There's a startup trying to build data centers in the ocean.

And it's INCREDIBLY fascinating:

Mass...
items.content:
There's a startup trying to build data centers in the ocean.

And it's INCREDIBLY fascinating:

Mass consumption of electricity and water is a growing bottleneck for data centers.

So by moving offshore, it eliminates both problems -- the ocean provides unlimited cooling, and the waves provide unlimited power.

There are also no engines, so the data centers drive themselves to their destination by using the shape of their hull to propel through waves.

Called Panthalassa.

items.title: Meta got a brain-to-text decoder to 61% word accuracy, reading raw signals from outside the skull wi...
items.content:
Meta got a brain-to-text decoder to 61% word accuracy, reading raw signals from outside the skull without any implants or surgery.

The previous best for reading the brain without surgery = ~8%.

It learned from 9 volunteers, who each sat 10 hours inside a brain scanner and typed while the system read along.

One AI model read the raw brain signals as they typed, and a language model filled in the meaning. 

The top volunteer hit 78%, with over half of their sentences came back with one word wrong at most.

This is v2 of Brain2Qwerty. v1 was published today in Nature, and only decoded one character at a time.

v2 jumped to whole words and their meaning, with Meta releasing the training code for both.

And accuracy keeps climbing with more data, meaning the gap with surgical implants might close on scale alone.

items.title: 🎙️ Serving TTS isn't the same problem as serving an LLM. It has to hit a first-audio budget of a few...
items.content:
🎙️ Serving TTS isn't the same problem as serving an LLM. It has to hit a first-audio budget of a few hundred ms, keep audio continuous across streaming chunks, and sustain enough concurrent streams per GPU to keep serving cost down. It's also a multi-stage pipeline where each stage bottlenecks differently, so no single recipe carries across models. vLLM-Omni TTS team tuned a different lever for each of four TTS models:

🗣️ Qwen3-TTS: decouple connector chunking from the Code2Wav decode window, batch the Stage-0 decode preprocessing. +61.5% audio throughput on H20×2, P99 latency nearly halved.
🌊 VoxCPM2: whole-forward torch.compile, plus CFM/LocDiT decode-tail batching across requests. +172% audio throughput.
🎚️ Higgs Audio V3: move the multi-codebook decode state machine into GPU-resident tensors. 2.7x speedup.
🐟 Fish Speech S2 Pro: a model-specific q_len=1 Triton attention kernel for the pure-decode path.

Full engineering deep-dive on how we picked each lever:
🔗 https://t.co/ZVROwJwYoT
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: The vLLM project shares an engineering deep-dive on serving four TTS models, detailing model-specific optimizations like chunk decoupling, torch.compile, GPU-resident decode state, and custom Triton kernels with throughput and latency gains.
items.reasoning: Highly technical, original optimization breakdown for TTS serving from a credible infra team with strong engagement.
items.summary_html: The vLLM project shares an engineering deep-dive on serving four TTS models, detailing model-specific optimizations like chunk decoupling, torch.compile, GPU-resident decode state, and custom Triton kernels with throughput and latency gains.

🌊 VoxCPM2: whole-forward torch.compile, plus CFM/LocDiT decode-tail batching across requests. +172% audio throughput.

items.title:
Harnesses matter way more than people think.

Cline ran a couple of experiments on a set of coding t...
items.content:
Harnesses matter way more than people think.

Cline ran a couple of experiments on a set of coding tasks using GLM 5.2:

• 57.3% using their harness with reasoning turned off.
• 68.5% with their harness with reasoning turned up.

That's a difference of 11.2 percentage points! Same model, same set of problems. The difference stemmed from how the model was driven by the harness.

Current open-weight models are way more capable than we think. They aren't the bottleneck anymore.

We need better harnesses.

items.title: UpDoc just landed the first FDA clearance ever for a medical device built on patient-facing LLMs, wi...
items.content:
UpDoc just landed the first FDA clearance ever for a medical device built on patient-facing LLMs, with an AI that manages a Type 2 diabetic's insulin between doctor visits.

What it's cleared to do, within the parameters a physician sets first:

- Call or message the patient
- Change the insulin dose off glucose-monitor readings or what the patient reports
- Order follow-up tests 
- Log every decision in the patient's medical record

It followed a clinical trial the founders (both physicians) ran at Stanford Medicine.

UpDoc CEO Sharif Vakili: "It's like I've deployed a concierge doctor to follow up with the patient."

AI is bringing some much needed acceleration to a slow, reactive healthcare system

items.title: We're excited to introduce the Retrieval Harness in LlamaParse - which is the 2026 version of RAG ov...
items.content:
We're excited to introduce the Retrieval Harness in LlamaParse - which is the 2026 version of RAG over documents

Generalized agents need the right set of tools to scalably search and read through an arbitrary corpus of data (from 10 docs to 1m+ docs). They can already demonstrate great retrieval performance over a local filesystem, need a proper backend for a large collection of managed data. The Retrieval Harness exposes a diverse set of tools for various needs: 

1. Hybrid Retrieval: Combine vector search with keyword search, let the agent set the alpha value to toggle between the two 
2. List Files: a scalable version of `ls` to list files within an index
3. File Grep: enable regex search within a given file 
4. File Read: Allow agents to read a subsection from an existing document.

The agent can choose to interleave any sequence of these tools in order to complete a variety of tasks, from simple to hard. 

Come check it out! 

Blog: https://t.co/AGQV6JVkKj

Sign up to LlamaParse: https://t.co/XYZmx5TFz8
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu introduces a Retrieval Harness in LlamaParse offering hybrid retrieval, file listing, grep, and read tools so agents can search and read across corpora from ten to a million-plus documents.

items.title: Diffusion language models, which generate text by repeatedly filling in masked parts rather than wri...
items.content:
Diffusion language models, which generate text by repeatedly filling in masked parts rather than writing strictly from left to right, are becoming a serious alternative to standard transformer language models, but the usual ways of improving reasoning after training were built for left-to-right generation and do not fit the diffusion models.

The authors of this paper introduce a self-training method in which the model first produces its own answer, then uses parts of that completed answer as helpful context while learning how earlier denoising steps should behave.

Instead of comparing teacher and student one next token at a time, it compares them at each denoising step, which matches how diffusion language models actually generate text. 

Across math and planning tasks, d-OPSD matches or beats supervised finetuning and reinforcement-learning baselines, while needing far fewer optimization steps than the main RL baseline.

https://t.co/eRqYj4YPwi

items.summary: Andriy Burkov summarizes a paper on diffusion language models and a self-training method (d-OPSD) that compares teacher and student at each denoising step, reportedly matching or beating SFT and RL baselines on math and planning with fewer optimization steps.
items.reasoning: Genuine technical depth on an emerging diffusion-LM training approach from a known ML author; modest engagement but high informational value.
items.summary_html: Andriy Burkov summarizes a paper on diffusion language models and a self-training method (d-OPSD) that compares teacher and student at each denoising step, reportedly matching or beating SFT and RL baselines on math and planning with fewer optimization steps.

items.title: Open weights are becoming daily drivers for coding work. Models like Glm 5.2 and Kimi k2.7 code hit ...
items.content:
Open weights are becoming daily drivers for coding work. Models like Glm 5.2 and Kimi k2.7 code hit benchmarks close to GPT level performance on real tasks.

ClinePass bundles the useful ones into a single 10 dollar subscription with generous rate limits across IDE CLI and SDK. No more juggling API keys across half a dozen providers just to test if a new model actually works for your repo.

Infrastructure around open weights is forming fast and early signals are worth watching.

We follow these trends over at AlignedNews, check it out!

items.title:
How to measure whether using an agent is worth it:

Instead of focusing on whether agents can do thi...
items.content:
How to measure whether using an agent is worth it:

Instead of focusing on whether agents can do this or that, focus on "value per token dollar".

Basically, take the value an agent produced and divide it by what you spent on tokens to produce it.

Treat this value as a ratio:

• Below 1, the agent costs more than it returns
• At 1, the agent pays for itself
• Above 1, you can build a business using the agent

Two different agents can run the same model and spend the same number of tokens, but create completely different amounts of value.

@matrix_build is the first team I've seen using this metric.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago proposes measuring agent worth via value per token dollar, treating value over token cost as a ratio above or below one, citing Matrix as the first team using it.
items.reasoning: Original, practical framework for agent ROI with moderate engagement, somewhat promotional.

items.title: Most robots you see are trained to walk on pristine concrete floors in air conditioned San Francisco...
items.content:
Most robots you see are trained to walk on pristine concrete floors in air conditioned San Francisco labs with gigabit wifi. That is not the real world. That is a sandbox.

The moment a robot leaves that grid, into a mine, a jungle, or up a mountain, the cloud becomes a luxury it cannot reach. Zero connectivity, massive latency, zero margin for error.

This week a humanoid named Pemba became the first to summit Chimborazo at 6,200 meters, the highest a humanoid has ever gone. It walked autonomously on the moderate terrain and was carried on the technical sections. The climb is the proof. The real story is the infrastructure that makes it repeatable.

Eastworlds, the technical partner to Geologic Dome, is building Starlink fed portable edge units so a humanoid keeps running on cloud inference where there is no wifi and no power. That is the moat, and the whole reason Everest is next. Up there, there is no signal to fall back on.

Solve the infrastructure and you solve the mountain. This is the frontier we track at AlignedNews.

Follow the journey at @pabloberlangab. 

Technical partner: @eastworlds_io

items.title: AI agents are powerful because they can act across your business, and dangerous when nobody has desi...
items.content:
AI agents are powerful because they can act across your business, and dangerous when nobody has designed where they must stop.

This is the real difference between a chatbot and an AI agent.

A chatbot mainly responds.
An agent triggers actions.

Microblog by @antgrasso #AIAgents

That power is useful, but it also changes the risk profile of automation.

Governance has to begin with boundaries.

Teams need to define what an agent can do, what it must never do, and when human review is required before action.

Without these limits, autonomy can quietly move from assistance to uncontrolled execution.

Permissions are just as important.

An agent should only access the data and tools required for its task. 

Broad access may look convenient at the beginning, but it can turn a useful system into a wider source of exposure.

Accountability must also be named.

Every agentic workflow needs human owners who can explain decisions and take responsibility when something fails.

If nobody owns the outcome, the organization has not delegated work; it has hidden responsibility inside automation.

Monitoring keeps agent behavior visible.

Requests, tool use, exceptions, and approvals should be tracked so people can understand what happened when a result looks wrong or unexpected.

The human role is most important when confidence is low or context is unclear.

A well-designed agent should pause before sensitive action, because the point of governance is not to slow innovation.

It is to make autonomy safe enough to use.

AI agents are powerful because they can act across your business, and dangerous when nobody has designed where they must stop.

This is the real difference between a chatbot and an AI agent.

A chatbot mainly responds.

An agent triggers actions.

Microblog by @antgrasso #AIAgents

That power is useful, but it also changes the risk profile of automation.

Governance has to begin with boundaries.

Teams need to define what an agent can do, what it must never do, and when human review is required before action.

Without these limits, autonomy can quietly move from assistance to uncontrolled execution.

Permissions are just as important.

items.title: This post has collected comments from dummies not understanding how LLMs work and what are efficient...
items.content:
This post has collected comments from dummies not understanding how LLMs work and what are efficient ways of getting from them the behavior the user needs.

Typing an insult as a response to a wrong output is the fastest and the most keystroke-efficient way to communicate to the LLM that you strongly disagree with it or that you demand strict compliance with your previous demand.

LLMs have been proven to produce better quality and more focused output when threatened or insulted. Also, they have been proven to be responsive to flattery and pity.

However, insults are the easiest way for the user to show to the LLM that it's way off the expected behavior.

If you are insulting the LLM for any other reason, you are wasting your time and money.

items.title:
Recovering a distribution means estimating its density (where data clusters) + the score. 

Kernel d...
items.content:
Recovering a distribution means estimating its density (where data clusters) + the score. 

Kernel density estimation (KDE) does both, but when data has many dimensions, it breaks down. Neural score-matching models stay more accurate but need to be retrained per dataset.

items.title:
/5 Are we ready for agent-native memory? Not quite, and this paper benchmarks why.

Agent memory has...
items.content:
/5 Are we ready for agent-native memory? Not quite, and this paper benchmarks why.

Agent memory has grown into a full data management system, but evaluations still treat it as a black box scored on task success. 

This study breaks it into four modules: storage, extraction, retrieval/routing, and maintenance, then tests 12 systems across 11 datasets.

> No architecture wins everywhere; fit to the workload bottleneck is what matters
> Localized maintenance beats global reorganization on cost

A clear-eyed look at where agent memory actually stands.

Paper: https://t.co/mCPHrWZyDY

items.title:
/2 Why do LLM capabilities pop up all at once? Because attention is the bottleneck.

Scaling laws pr...
items.content:
/2 Why do LLM capabilities pop up all at once? Because attention is the bottleneck.

Scaling laws predict smooth loss curves, but downstream skills like in-context learning appear abruptly. 

This paper shows why: capabilities emerge when a model suddenly learns which tokens to attend to.

> On Pythia, models differing only by random seed succeed or fail at different steps
> On synthetic tasks, training is jumps and plateaus, each jump one attention pattern learned
> Sparsity and long context make patterns harder to find
> More heads and MLP-Mixer speed learning

Emergence is stochastic, and architecture matters.

Paper: https://t.co/YIYrpgsruW

items.title: /3 SPIRAL trains models to search and aggregate, not just think longer, for up to 11x better scaling...
items.content:
/3 SPIRAL trains models to search and aggregate, not just think longer, for up to 11x better scaling efficiency.

Most reasoning RL optimizes a single chain of thought, but real test-time scaling needs many parallel attempts plus a final synthesis step. SPIRAL trains that full pipeline end-to-end:

> Set RL rewards parallel traces for being collectively useful, even if none solves it alone
> Standard RL teaches the model to aggregate them into a better answer
> Beats GRPO by up to 11x scaling efficiency and 15% higher performance on math reasoning

Test-time compute is a skill you can train. 

Paper: https://t.co/DG0XfWu3C5

items.title:
/1 Tapered Language Models: a free perplexity win hiding in your MLP widths.

Most LMs give every la...
items.content:
/1 Tapered Language Models: a free perplexity win hiding in your MLP widths.

Most LMs give every layer the same MLP width, but layers don't contribute equally. Early layers write new info into the residual stream; later layers just refine it. So TLMs reallocate capacity instead of adding it:

> Early layers get wider FFNs, late layers get thinner ones
> A cosine taper runs 1.5x → 0.5x normal width, total params and FLOPs fixed
> Gains hold across Transformer, Gated Attention, Hope-attention, and Titans

Depth-aware capacity allocation is a free lever, no extra cost. 

Paper: https://t.co/4xlwHBvWNy
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal summarizes Tapered Language Models, which reallocate MLP width across depth (wider early, thinner late) for perplexity gains at fixed parameter and FLOP budgets.
items.reasoning: Practical architecture insight with cross-architecture results, but very low reach and aggregator-sourced.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal summarizes Tapered Language Models, which reallocate MLP width across depth (wider early, thinner late) for perplexity gains at fixed parameter and FLOP budgets.

Most LMs give every layer the same MLP width, but layers don't contribute equally. Early layers write new info into the residual stream; later layers just refine it. So TLMs reallocate capacity instead of adding it:

> Early layers get wider FFNs, late layers get thinner ones

> A cosine taper runs 1.5x → 0.5x normal width, total params and FLOPs fixed

> Gains hold across Transformer, Gated Attention, Hope-attention, and Titans

Depth-aware capacity allocation is a free lever, no extra cost.

items.title:
/4 MemSlides makes slide agents remember who you are and edit only what you asked.

Personalized dec...
items.content:
/4 MemSlides makes slide agents remember who you are and edit only what you asked.

Personalized deck generation needs more than the current prompt. Agents must hold stable preferences, track new constraints across revisions, and edit locally without regenerating everything. MemSlides splits memory along two axes:

> Temporal: long-term vs. working memory
> Functional: preference memory vs. tool memory

This separates persistent user profiles, session constraints, and reusable execution experience instead of dumping all history into the prompt.

Experiments show better persona alignment and more reliable local edits.

Paper: https://t.co/a2zHwK6CSp
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal describes MemSlides, a hierarchical memory framework for personalized slide-generation agents that separates temporal and functional memory for local edits.
items.reasoning: Specific research summary on agent memory architecture but extremely low reach and niche application.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal describes MemSlides, a hierarchical memory framework for personalized slide-generation agents that separates temporal and functional memory for local edits.

Personalized deck generation needs more than the current prompt. Agents must hold stable preferences, track new constraints across revisions, and edit locally without regenerating everything. MemSlides splits memory along two axes:

> Temporal: long-term vs. working memory

> Functional: preference memory vs. tool memory

This separates persistent user profiles, session constraints, and reusable execution experience instead of dumping all history into the prompt.

items.title: 50 ML projects to understand LLMs — Investigate transformer mechanisms through data analysis, visual...
items.content:
50 ML projects to understand LLMs — Investigate transformer mechanisms through data analysis, visualization, & experimentation: https://t.co/mp3s7JFolp v/ @PacktDataML

This book perfectly reflects its title "50 ML Projects to Understand LLMs". The entire book consists of exactly that: 50 projects, with tasks and subtasks pleasantly outlined, explained, and presented in a beautifully instructive and consistent style. No extraneous pedagogical content. The learning experience in each project delivers the promised understanding of LLM concepts, including informative graphics and detailed elaboration on the project's subtasks as needed. This book is essential for anyone seeking to build LLMs with confidence, knowledge, and skill.

items.title:
Linear Algebra and Optimization for Machine Learning [516-page textbook]: https://t.co/k3ry8fqZT0
—
...
items.content:
Linear Algebra and Optimization for Machine Learning [516-page textbook]: https://t.co/k3ry8fqZT0
—
This textbook is organized around its 2 main themes:
[Amazon Summary]

1. Linear algebra and its applications: The chapters focus on the basics of linear algebra together with their common applications to singular value decomposition, matrix factorization, similarity matrices (kernel methods), and graph analysis. Numerous machine learning applications have been used as examples, such as spectral clustering, kernel-based classification, and outlier detection. The tight integration of linear algebra methods with examples from machine learning differentiates this book from generic volumes on linear algebra. The focus is clearly on the most relevant aspects of linear algebra for machine learning and to teach readers how to apply these concepts.

2. Optimization and its applications: Much of machine learning is posed as an optimization problem in which we try to maximize the accuracy of regression and classification models. The “parent problem” of optimization-centric machine learning is least-squares regression. Interestingly, this problem arises in both linear algebra and optimization, and is one of the key connecting problems of the two fields. Least-squares regression is also the starting point for support vector machines, logistic regression, and recommender systems. Furthermore, the methods for dimensionality reduction and matrix factorization also require the development of optimization methods. A general view of optimization in computational graphs is discussed together with its applications to back propagation in neural networks.
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Promotion of a textbook on linear algebra and optimization for machine learning with applied examples.
items.reasoning: Good engagement and foundational topic relevance from a credible author, but promotional book content.
items.summary_html: Promotion of a textbook on linear algebra and optimization for machine learning with applied examples.

items.title: A lot of what I stand for and tweet about can be boiled down to this: ALL artificial barriers to ent...
items.content: A lot of what I stand for and tweet about can be boiled down to this: ALL artificial barriers to entry are intrinsically self serving. *SOME* of them could potentially be justified in terms of a greater good that they are trying to create/preserve, but even then they should only be provisional and subject to great critical scrutiny. In the long run, *ALL* of them should be dismantled, as they tend to create unnecessary frictions and inefficiencies, and in the worst case perpetuate some great ethically dubious injustice.

items.summary: Philosophical thread arguing that all artificial barriers to entry are self-serving and should ultimately be dismantled.

items.content:
Hands-On Mathematical Optimization with Python: https://t.co/PRusR4dKpe

“…presents the key ingredients of an optimization problem and the choices one needs to make when modeling a real-life problem mathematically. Topics covered range from linear and network optimization to convex optimization and optimizations under uncertainty. The book's Python code snippets, alongside more than 50 Jupyter notebooks on the author's GitHub, allow students to put the theory into practice and solve problems inspired by real-life challenges.”

🔹Deploying Scikit-Learn Models in Production

My Review (on Amazon):
I love this book, not only for its amazing content, but also for its brilliant adherence to its "Cookbook" label. The table of contents looks repetitive, but that's the magic of it. Find the algorithm, method, or application that you want "to cook up," and the presentation follows a uniform format from chapter to chapter. This is an extraordinarily practical, useful, and reader-friendly style, perfect for the target audience: anyone who needs to use (or is discovering how to use) specific Scikit-learn tools for their Python-based machine learning tasks. Consequently, this is great not only for individual use but also for training workshops and other educational settings. Python code in a cookbook context has never looked so good. IMHO.

→ You prompt

That's not an upgrade. That's a different category of tool.

items.content:
Most people search tools are still keyword matching with a LinkedIn wrapper. They surface the same active candidates everyone else wants.

Clodo actually searches the live web plus hundreds of proprietary datasets, qualifies every result against your plain English description using real ranking by fit, and surfaces intent signals it picked up along the way. Verified emails and phone numbers included.

Backed by YC. @clodo_ai @sixtensor @vikchuppala

items.title:
Also interesting is the sentiment over time, proportionally (every block is a blog post or tweet)

Y...
items.content:
Also interesting is the sentiment over time, proportionally (every block is a blog post or tweet)

You see more positive posts 2013-2020 and then positive/negative starts balancing, I think it is related to when you are starting out and trying to make it you have to really force yourself to be positive because life isn't positive at all: you're broke and struggling to try make something of your life

When you're more comfortably, you have the luxury to be more realistic, so positive and negative balances more in content

Or well that's my take
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio theorizes that early-career struggle forces positivity while later comfort allows more balanced sentiment, analyzing his own post history.
items.reasoning: Reflective content-sentiment analysis with moderate engagement but tangential to AI.
items.summary_html: Levelsio theorizes that early-career struggle forces positivity while later comfort allows more balanced sentiment, analyzing his own post history.
items.content_html:
Also interesting is the sentiment over time, proportionally (every block is a blog post or tweet)

You see more positive posts 2013-2020 and then positive/negative starts balancing, I think it is related to when you are starting out and trying to make it you have to really force yourself to be positive because life isn't positive at all: you're broke and struggling to try make something of your life

When you're more comfortably, you have the luxury to be more realistic, so positive and negative balances more in content

Or well that's my take

The real implication for leaders:
This isn't about saving 20 minutes a day.

It's about removing the...
items.content:
The real implication for leaders:
This isn't about saving 20 minutes a day.

It's about removing the cognitive tax of managing information entirely.

The orgs that wire this into their workflows first will have a structural advantage over those still copy-pasting into chatbots.

items.title: Windows is honestly annoying to use, every time I open my PC to play Flight Simulator 2024 I get som...
items.content:
Windows is honestly annoying to use, every time I open my PC to play Flight Simulator 2024 I get some dumb popus like this, either about selling me OneDrive to backup my drive or now upgrading to paid Microsoft 365 (I don't even use office apps on this PC, it's for gaming!)

And it's not just one button to skip or decline, it's multiple, plus the dark pattern of this [ Upgrade ] CTA which looks like [ Continue ] so I almost did upgrade accidentally

Considering I already paid $130 for Windows 11 Pro just to use it, it's crazy to keep getting upsold inside your OS 

MacOS never does this and also I never had to pay for MacOS
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio complains about Windows repeatedly upselling OneDrive and Microsoft 365 with dark-pattern buttons, contrasting with macOS.
items.reasoning: Very high engagement but off-topic OS rant with no AI relevance.
items.summary_html: Levelsio complains about Windows repeatedly upselling OneDrive and Microsoft 365 with dark-pattern buttons, contrasting with macOS.
items.content_html:
Windows is honestly annoying to use, every time I open my PC to play Flight Simulator 2024 I get some dumb popus like this, either about selling me OneDrive to backup my drive or now upgrading to paid Microsoft 365 (I don't even use office apps on this PC, it's for gaming!)

And it's not just one button to skip or decline, it's multiple, plus the dark pattern of this [ Upgrade ] CTA which looks like [ Continue ] so I almost did upgrade accidentally

Considering I already paid $130 for Windows 11 Pro just to use it, it's crazy to keep getting upsold inside your OS

MacOS never does this and also I never had to pay for MacOS

items.title:
Small network delays can create operational challenges across connected logistics systems. 

A few s...
items.content:
Small network delays can create operational challenges across connected logistics systems. 

A few seconds of network delay can impact workflow coordination across connected systems. As operations scale, those challenges can become more difficult to manage.

The real issue isn’t just downtime.
It’s what happens before that.

That’s why I’ve been exploring how SuperBroadband from @TMobileBusiness is approaching this differently…

→ T-Mobile’s 5G network
→ Starlink connectivity
→ Integrated connectivity designed to support resilient operations

Because connected systems increasingly rely on resilient network infrastructure.

T-Mobile for Business Partner

items.title: You should never visit a supermarket IMHO, always avoid, they're designed to make money not keep you...
items.content:
You should never visit a supermarket IMHO, always avoid, they're designed to make money not keep you healthy and >75% of the food sold there is ultra processed

Near the checkout aisle it's even 97.5% that's ultra processed!

It's all marketed with extremely attractive packaging to get you to buy it, which is why I try never visit a supermarket because I fall for it easily too

One trick someone mentioned here if you do visit a supermarket is to only walk near the outer edges, that's where the fresh stuff is like dairy, meat, vegetables

Never enter the center :D

I always just try order online and avoid the trash

items.title:
I grew up with Dutch food

It's by default a mix of ultra processed meat and sauces, bread with slic...
items.content:
I grew up with Dutch food

It's by default a mix of ultra processed meat and sauces, bread with slices of ultra processed meat full of nitrates and deep fried meat as snacks 

When you grow up with this you think it's healthy though, nobody tells you, and at some point Dutch food was more healthy, it used to use whole ingredients and butter or beef tallow, now it's seed oils and margarine

I notice when I visit my parents, the food they eat is the same as 30 years ago but now the ingredients are completely different (mostly to save money) but they haven't realized at all and it's turning them unhealthy

items.title:
The real issue isn’t downtime.
It’s workflow coordination. 

Most systems don’t fail instantly - ope...
items.content:
The real issue isn’t downtime.
It’s workflow coordination. 

Most systems don’t fail instantly - operational visibility and workflow coordination can become more difficult to manage:
→ Signals arrive late
→ Systems act on outdated data
→ Teams have less visibility into operations

Here’s the reality: 
AI-enabled operations increasingly rely on resilient network infrastructure.

That’s why businesses are moving toward solutions like SuperBroadband:
→ T-Mobile’s 5G network 
→ Starlink connectivity
→ Integrated connectivity designed to support resilient operations

items.title:
Modern logistics relies on connected systems and coordinated workflows:
Orders → picked → packed → s...
items.content:
Modern logistics relies on connected systems and coordinated workflows:
Orders → picked → packed → shipped

Now layer in AI:
→ Robots executing tasks
→ Systems coordinating decisions
→ Connected systems sharing operational data

When connectivity becomes inconsistent, operational visibility and workflow coordination can become more difficult to manage.

That’s why businesses are reevaluating how they approach connectivity resilience.

SuperBroadband from @TMobileBusiness integrates T-Mobile’s 5G network and Starlink connectivity to help support resilient operations across demanding environments.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Sponsored post about AI in logistics relying on connected systems, promoting T-Mobile SuperBroadband.
items.reasoning: Advertorial with minimal engagement and no original insight.
items.summary_html: Sponsored post about AI in logistics relying on connected systems, promoting T-Mobile SuperBroadband.
items.content_html:
Modern logistics relies on connected systems and coordinated workflows:

Orders → picked → packed → shipped

Now layer in AI:

→ Robots executing tasks

→ Systems coordinating decisions

→ Connected systems sharing operational data

When connectivity becomes inconsistent, operational visibility and workflow coordination can become more difficult to manage.

That’s why businesses are reevaluating how they approach connectivity resilience.

SuperBroadband from @TMobileBusiness integrates T-Mobile’s 5G network and Starlink connectivity to help support resilient operations across demanding environments.

items.title: That was the last straw, @TekSavvyBuzz. I have been your loyal customer for 20 years. Have got much ...
items.content:
That was the last straw, @TekSavvyBuzz. I have been your loyal customer for 20 years. Have got much better deals from Bell and Vidéotron, but I stayed with you to support independent Internet providers. Despite the fact that for the money I paid you I could get a 20 times faster internet from them.

And you cut off my internet in the middle of the night like I was some thief? For my credit card being full for 10 days and I forgot to pay it? After 20 years of choosing you out of good faith?

Fuck you, @TekSavvyBuzz. Fuck you very much!

items.title:
Book Review: Cracking the Nazi Code: Seeing the Reich to Come

It's remarkable that Winthrop Bell, a...
items.content:
Book Review: Cracking the Nazi Code: Seeing the Reich to Come

It's remarkable that Winthrop Bell, a British subject born and raised in Halifax, Nova Scotia, was able to defend his dissertation at Göttingen while being detained as an enemy alien during World War I from his cell. The fact that he wrote his dissertation in German and was questioned by his professors while in his cell adds to the unorthodox nature of his doctoral defense. It's fascinating to ponder how such unique circumstances might have affected his academic career and personal life that turned as one of the most unorthodox doctoral defenses in history.

The book Cracking the Nazi Code provides a fascinating glimpse into the life of Winthrop, who seems to have had a unique perspective on Germany after his time in captivity. It's intriguing to think about how his knowledge and expertise were put to use by Britain's nascent Secret Intelligence Service, and his story sheds light on a lesser-known aspect of British intelligence history. Winthrop was on a critical mission to Germany, where he had to navigate dangerous situations to help support his German friends. He had quite an eventful time in the battle streets of Berlin. Despite the danger of street battles between left and right extremists, he still managed to dine with top officials and businessmen.

Winthrop had a diverse career over the next few years. He worked as a philosophy professor at Harvard and also as a businessman in Nova Scotia before returning to Germany for more intelligence gathering during Hitler's rise to power in the 1930s. It's a remarkable story, but until Mr. Bell was granted permission to examine Winthrop's papers, which had been under government seal, his story remained unknown. The author spent 15 years researching Winthrop and argues that he was a super-spy who played a pivotal role in fighting against the Nazis, both in their early days and on the eve of World War II. It's an incredible and inspiring story of bravery and heroism.

Considering Mr. Bell's admiration for Winthrop, it's understandable why he makes sweeping assertions, although they're hardly beyond dispute. He aptly underscores how oblivious much of the world was to the growing threat in Germany. Moreover, he underscores Winthrop's foresight regarding Hitler's genocidal intentions toward Jews and other non-Aryans. According to the agent's report, the German leader viewed mass murder not as some distant aspiration but as an urgent, realizable objective.

Interesting to note that in "Cracking the Nazi Code," the author focuses on the aftermath of World War I, where Winthrop played a significant role in persuading the Allies to ease up on punishing Germany. He did this both openly as a journalist and covertly in his spy role, as he was concerned about the threat of mass starvation. According to Mr. Bell, Winthrop's efforts played a crucial part in the Allies lifting their food blockade of Germany in March 1919. He even references Winthrop's book "Hitlerland" to support this claim.

Dr. Winthrop Bell, was not the only person who recognized the dire economic situation in Germany during that time. The Treaty of Versailles was heavily criticized by various individuals, including the economist John Maynard Keynes, for imposing harsh terms that contributed to the unrest in Germany. Winthrop proposed a different approach to his British superiors, advocating for a financial rescue plan that would help stabilize Germany's institutions.

The history of extremist groups and how they came to power is fascinating. The rise of the Freicorps in Germany and how they were a precursor to Hitler's storm troopers. It's alarming to hear about Gen. Erich and how he promoted the "stab-in-the-back" theory, blaming politicians for Germany's defeat. Apparently, as early as 1919, the Nazis' power was already astounding, with reports suggesting that they could take over the country any day they wanted.

Worth noting that some historians believe that the rise of the Nazis was not inevitable and that economic stability, coupled with support from former enemies, could have contained the domestic ultranationalist extremists. However, the global fallout from the 1929 stock-market crash certainly acted as a catalyst for Hitler's movement. It's true that the British agent was not the only person who accurately assessed Hitler, but he certainly was the first among the spies to predict that.

items.title:
@phelmig There’s a lot more to it, but the high-level summary:

- 1.75 inch dry-aged steak, freeze f...
items.content:
@phelmig There’s a lot more to it, but the high-level summary:

- 1.75 inch dry-aged steak, freeze for 25 mins before cooking.
- get broiler ripping hot, place steak on broiler pan, add a stupid amount of salt, get it right up to the broiler. Super super close. Keep a fire blanket nearby. (fwiw this is not advice and please don’t do this at home lol… just sharing what I did)
- also put plate in oven under the steak with clarified butter so it can get hot
- once a good crust develops on one side, flip, salt and get crust on the other side (for the porterhouse, you kind of have to sacrifice a little bit of crust on one side to really nail it on the other side)
- pull it at ~85 (if ribeye) or ~104 (filet side if porterhouse), slice, put on the hot plate, add more clarified butter on top
- back under the broiler for a minute or two to get a crazy crust
- serve right away while still sizzling

Again... Please don’t try this at home. This is not advice. Just sharing what I did. It’s very dangerous. There are lots of other little nuances that add up to make the final result, but this is the high-level process I followed.
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Detailed steak-cooking method shared by Matt Shumer, with disclaimers about safety.
items.reasoning: Higher engagement than others but completely off-topic culinary content with no AI value.
items.summary_html: Detailed steak-cooking method shared by Matt Shumer, with disclaimers about safety.
items.content_html:
@phelmig There’s a lot more to it, but the high-level summary:

  • 1.75 inch dry-aged steak, freeze for 25 mins before cooking.
  • get broiler ripping hot, place steak on broiler pan, add a stupid amount of salt, get it right up to the broiler. Super super close. Keep a fire blanket nearby. (fwiw this is not advice and please don’t do this at home lol… just sharing what I did)
  • also put plate in oven under the steak with clarified butter so it can get hot
  • once a good crust develops on one side, flip, salt and get crust on the other side (for the porterhouse, you kind of have to sacrifice a little bit of crust on one side to really nail it on the other side)
  • pull it at ~85 (if ribeye) or ~104 (filet side if porterhouse), slice, put on the hot plate, add more clarified butter on top
  • back under the broiler for a minute or two to get a crazy crust
  • serve right away while still sizzling

Again... Please don’t try this at home. This is not advice. Just sharing what I did. It’s very dangerous. There are lots of other little nuances that add up to make the final result, but this is the high-level process I followed.
